2: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum L.), a popular and widely grown culinary vegetable, is a major component of the Mediterranean diet (Beecher, 1998). Organically grown tomato is favored for con- sumption in salads, and other cuisines due to their flavor, food safety and positive human health effects (Gravel et al., 2010; Dias et al., 2017). When outdoor cultivation is not feasible in countries

such as Canada due to the extremely cold temperatures, greenhouse-based productions form the best alternative to continue with the market demand-supply. However, commercial greenhouse vegetable production needs extensive application of microclimate controllers such as temperature, chemical fertilizers, and pesticides. Application of these parameters, especially chemi- cal fertilizers leads to greenhouse gas (GHGs) emissions, mainly as carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N20) which contribute to global warming (Ntinas et al., 2017). Therefore, greenhouse vegetable production increases damage to the envi- ronment and human health, thereby risking food security and safety (Tringovska et al., 2015). On the other hand, the increased cost of raw materials to produce chemical fertilizers is on the rise; thus, the current agriculture sector has to face new challenges to find alternative sources to supplement mineral fertilizers (Nogueira et al., 2010).

In line with this fact, as well as increasing consumer interests in healthier food and the need for conserving environment and biodiversity, organic agriculture has been widely considered during the last several decades (Willer and Lernoud, 2016). One of the oldest methods of supplying nutrients and also improving chemical and physical properties of the soil is the application of organic matter such as manure and green-fertilizers (Aranda et al., 2015). Despite the highly desirable characteristics of manure and other organic matters, their use may lead to some problems due to the large volume to weight ratio. Also, depending on their nature, they can be a good basis for the dispersal of plant pests and diseases.

In order to solve these problems, researchers are trying to change the model of the use of organic matter as fertilizers. Accordingly, the extraction of nutrients from organic matter and their usage as a soluble component could be a promising idea in improving the application of organic matter. However, the organic matter should contain high levels of nutrients, especially nitrogen, and such extractions should be both technically and economically feasible and viable. In line with that, the utilization of organic matter based on nitrogen-fixing plants for organic fertilizers pro- duction seems to be a good choice and the use of Azolla is one such possibility (Maham et al., 2018).

Azolla, also known as water velvet, feathered mosquito fern, duckweed, or water fern, is popular for its bioremediatory potential and its quick biomass production (Miranda et al., 2016). It grows efficiently in nitrogen deficient medium because of the nitrogen- fixing capacity of its natural endophytic cyanobacterium symbi- ont, Anabaena azollae Strasburger. (Pereira and Vasconcelos, 2014). Its growth is prolific and can fix (N 2 nitrogen (biological N fixation - BNF) at the rate of 30-100 kg N ha crop (Yao et al., 2018). CO2 and water are utilized by Azolla to produce oxygen and mono saccharides in the chloroplast, thereby acting as a natural recycler, and subsequently lowering CO2 emissions (Wang et al., 2015). Hence, Azolla considered as one of the most significant organic fertilizers in agriculture (Gomes et al., 2018), could provide needed greenhouse fertilizer products. However, any new approach to organic fertilizer production and use requires careful consideration, especially with regard to environmental sustainability.

From a human health perspective, tomato contains high con- centrations of antioxidants such as vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) and carotenoids. The regular consumption of tomato and tomato-based products have been shown to reduce the risk of chronic health is- sues such as cardiovascular diseases (Willcox et al., 2003), and help decrease oxidative damage in tissues (Cilla et al., 2008). When grown in the greenhouse, tomato fruits typically have low levels of vitamin C due to artificial lighting, since natural light is the major regulator for vitamin C levels in plants (Ntagkas et al., 2016). Therefore, the amount of vitamin C produced in greenhouse to- matoes should be monitored under organic growth conditions. Also, as a new approach, the environmental impacts of each unit of natural vitamin C can also be estimated. Subsequently, the results obtained from this estimate will be used as a reference to compare the environmental impacts of the production of natural vitamin C, compared with the industrial production of vitamin C, in future research.

There is a range of methods and techniques to study greenhouse vegetable production systems and their behavior in creating envi- ronmental impacts, such as the standard life cycle assessment (LCA), that are essential tools in the improvement of sustainable production (Schröder et al., 2003; Chauhan et al., 2011). In the LCA analysis, the overall process of the production is analyzed consid- ering all the inputs and their interactions for awareness of envi- ronmental impacts at every stage of the production (Lazzerini et al., 2016). According to the comprehensiveness of LCA, various studies have been carried out on the environmental assessment of tomato

production in the greenhouse to identify environmental hot spots and to find solutions to eliminate them. Also, in some studies, environmental impacts based on conventional and organic tomato production has been compared using LCA. For example, in a study in China, the environmental effects of the production per tonne of organic and conventional tomato production in urban greenhouses showed that use of manure, instead of chemical fertilizers, as well as non-use of pesticides helped improve the environmental dam- age caused, per tonne tomato, in organic greenhouse production (He et al., 2016). Bosona and Gebresenbet's (2018) study in Sweden greenhouse suggested that when using manure as fertilizer, the damage to the environment was more related to heating and en- ergy for irrigation during the production.

This study was therefore carried out to assess the environmental performance of organic greenhouse tomatoes and its vitamin C based on processed organic fertilizers (herein organic fertilizers derived from Azolla, a nitrogen-fixing plant used for crop produc- tion) to identify environmental hotspots and suggest ways to decrease them.

Also, depending on their nature, they can be a good basis for the dispersal of plant pests and diseases.

In order to solve these problems, researchers are trying to change the model of the use of organic matter as fertilizers. Accordingly, the extraction of nutrients from organic matter and their usage as a soluble component could be a promising idea in improving the application of organic matter. However, the organic matter should contain high levels of nutrients, especially nitrogen, and such extractions should be both technically and economically feasible and viable. In line with that, the utilization of organic matter based on nitrogen-fixing plants for organic fertilizers pro- duction seems to be a good choice and the use of Azolla is one such possibility (Maham et al., 2018).

Azolla, also known as water velvet, feathered mosquito fern, duckweed, or water fern, is popular for its bioremediatory potential and its quick biomass production (Miranda et al., 2016). It grows efficiently in nitrogen deficient medium because of the nitrogen- fixing capacity of its natural endophytic cyanobacterium symbi- ont, Anabaena azollae Strasburger. (Pereira and Vasconcelos, 2014). Its growth is prolific and can fix (N 2 nitrogen (biological N fixation - BNF) at the rate of 30-100 kg N ha crop (Yao et al., 2018). CO2 and water are utilized by Azolla to produce oxygen and mono saccharides in the chloroplast, thereby acting as a natural recycler, and subsequently lowering CO2 emissions (Wang et al., 2015). Hence, Azolla considered as one of the most significant organic fertilizers in agriculture (Gomes et al., 2018), could provide needed greenhouse fertilizer products. However, any new approach to organic fertilizer production and use requires careful consideration, especially with regard to environmental sustainability.

From a human health perspective, tomato contains high con- centrations of antioxidants such as vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) and carotenoids. The regular consumption of tomato and tomato-based products have been shown to reduce the risk of chronic health is- sues such as cardiovascular diseases (Willcox et al., 2003), and help decrease oxidative damage in tissues (Cilla et al., 2008). When grown in the greenhouse, tomato fruits typically have low levels of vitamin C due to artificial lighting, since natural light is the major regulator for vitamin C levels in plants (Ntagkas et al., 2016). Therefore, the amount of vitamin C produced in greenhouse to- matoes should be monitored under organic growth conditions. Also, as a new approach, the environmental impacts of each unit of natural vitamin C can also be estimated. Subsequently, the results obtained from this estimate will be used as a reference to compare the environmental impacts of the production of natural vitamin C, compared with the industrial production of vitamin C, in future research.

production in the greenhouse to identify environmental hot spots and to find solutions to eliminate them. 10: The study site was a 196 greenhouse, located in Ste-Anne Bellevue, in Québec, Canada (Fig. 1). Quebec climate is continen- tal, with warm summers, cold snowy winters and high rainfall. Data collected by the Environment Canada meteorological station in Sainte Anne de Bellevue reports average January temperatures as -10.2 and July temperatures as 20.9 with the annual average temperature being 6.4 °C. The area experiences annual average precipitation of 1114 mm that includes 242 mm of snow (Gagnon et al., 2017). Because of poor natural light and high heating costs, generally tomato production in greenhouses in Quebec area is limited to spring (January-July) and fall (August-November) (Dorais et al., 1991).
11: Tomato cultivation was conducted in a research greenhouse of Macdonald Campus, McGill University, QC, Canada between 25 January 2017 and 25 May 2017. Tomato cultivation (Fig. 2) included the following methods: transplanting tomato seedlings in plastic pots containing soil and manure, organic fertilizer application, pest and disease control, irrigation, hand-pruning and harvesting. The plants were grown using the following temperature settings - maximum temperature: 23 °C, minimum temperature: 16 °C,
Fig. 1. Location of the area studied in Quebec, Canada.
Fig. 2. The system boundary production of packed tomato.

Seeds were sown in plastic trays, watered at regular intervals and fertilizers applied when needed. Given looming water con- sumption issues associated with climate change, drought stress was applied at 4 levels (100, 80, 40 and 20%). Electricity for venti- lation, lighting and heating were considered as the major compo- nents of climate control in the greenhouse. The tomato seedlings, planted in greenhouses, were transplanted in January to model their production based on those used for standard commercial production.

It should be noted that energy and other inputs associated with the greenhouse infrastructure include building materials and their maintenance to wear and tear over time. While the structure of the greenhouse is predominantly aluminum and glass, it was assumed that the used aluminum and other metals were from virgin mate- rials; and the greenhouse life span was 30 years, excluding the glass fittings. At the harvest, tomatoes were packed in containers made of polyethylene with a capacity to carry about 260g of fresh to- matoes. It should be noted that control of pests with chemical pesticides has not been carried out.
2.3. Fertilizer production based on a nitrogen-fixing plant (Azolla)"

Organic fertilizer used in this study was produced from Azolla at three different levels, 100, 66 and 33 percent of pure extract, diluted with distilled water. The extract was prepared by the following procedure. Fresh Azolla biomass was cleaned by several washes in water. The clean plants were washed using double distilled water and air dried in the shade (25 °C) for three weeks. The dried and powdered material (50g) was subjected to methanol (300 mL) extraction for 241 at a temperature just below the boiling point of the solvent. The filtrate was concentrated under vacuum at 30 °C using a rotary evaporator (Hahnshin Scientific Co.) and the concentrate stored at 4 °C for further use (modified method of

One of the main goals of this study was the environmental assessment of the production of each unit of vitamin C in tomatoes, and hence vitamin C was quantified in the tomato fruits using a modification of the method of Kapur et al. (2012). For 5 g of sample, 25 mL of metaphosphoric acid - acetic acid solution was added and homogenized in a 50 mL volumetric flask. The homogenate volume was increased to 50 mL by adding appropriate amounts of meta- phosphoric acid-acetic acid solution and the solution was centri- fuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min and the supernatant filtered. The supernatant solution was used to determine vitamin C content in tomato spectrophotometrically (Ultraspec 4300 pro, UV-visible spectrophotometer). For 4 mL of the supernatant, 0.23 mL of 3% bromine water was added to oxidizeascorbic acid to dehy- droascorbic acid. This was followed by adding 0.13 mL of 10% thiourea in order to remove excess bromine. Subsequently, 1 mL of 2, 4-DNPH solution was added, and all the standards, samples and blank solutions were incubated at 37 °C temperature for h in a water bath (Isotemp 120, Fisher Scientific, Canada), to check for the formation of osazone. After cooling the samples in an ice bath for 30 min, the samples were treated with 5 mL of chilled 85% H2SO4, with constant stirring. The resultant red color complex of osazone was recorded at 521 nm.

After cooling the samples in an ice bath for 30 min, the samples were treated with 5 mL of chilled 85% H2SO4, with constant stirring. The resultant red color complex of osazone was recorded at 521 nm.
14: Tomato cultivation was conducted in a research greenhouse of Macdonald Campus, McGill University, QC, Canada between 25 January 2017 and 25 May 2017. Tomato cultivation (Fig. 2) included the following methods: transplanting tomato seedlings in plastic pots containing soil and manure, organic fertilizer application, pest and disease control, irrigation, hand-pruning and harvesting. The plants were grown using the following temperature settings - maximum temperature: 23 °C, minimum temperature: 16 °C,
Fig. 1. Location of the area studied in Quebec, Canada.
Fig. 2. The system boundary production of packed tomato.

Seeds were sown in plastic trays, watered at regular intervals and fertilizers applied when needed. Given looming water con- sumption issues associated with climate change, drought stress was applied at 4 levels (100, 80, 40 and 20%). Electricity for venti- lation, lighting and heating were considered as the major compo- nents of climate control in the greenhouse. The tomato seedlings, planted in greenhouses, were transplanted in January to model their production based on those used for standard commercial production.

It should be noted that energy and other inputs associated with the greenhouse infrastructure include building materials and their maintenance to wear and tear over time. While the structure of the greenhouse is predominantly aluminum and glass, it was assumed that the used aluminum and other metals were from virgin mate- rials; and the greenhouse life span was 30 years, excluding the glass fittings. At the harvest, tomatoes were packed in containers made of polyethylene with a capacity to carry about 260g of fresh to- matoes. It should be noted that control of pests with chemical pesticides has not been carried out.
2.3. Fertilizer production based on a nitrogen-fixing plant (Azolla)"

Organic fertilizer used in this study was produced from Azolla at three different levels, 100, 66 and 33 percent of pure extract, diluted with distilled water. The extract was prepared by the following procedure. Fresh Azolla biomass was cleaned by several washes in water. The clean plants were washed using double distilled water and air dried in the shade (25 °C) for three weeks. The dried and powdered material (50g) was subjected to methanol (300 mL) extraction for 241 at a temperature just below the boiling point of the solvent. The filtrate was concentrated under vacuum at 30 °C using a rotary evaporator (Hahnshin Scientific Co.) and the concentrate stored at 4 °C for further use (modified method of

One of the main goals of this study was the environmental assessment of the production of each unit of vitamin C in tomatoes, and hence vitamin C was quantified in the tomato fruits using a modification of the method of Kapur et al. (2012). For 5 g of sample, 25 mL of metaphosphoric acid - acetic acid solution was added and homogenized in a 50 mL volumetric flask. The homogenate volume was increased to 50 mL by adding appropriate amounts of meta- phosphoric acid-acetic acid solution and the solution was centri- fuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min and the supernatant filtered. The supernatant solution was used to determine vitamin C content in tomato spectrophotometrically (Ultraspec 4300 pro, UV-visible spectrophotometer). For 4 mL of the supernatant, 0.23 mL of 3% bromine water was added to oxidizeascorbic acid to dehy- droascorbic acid. This was followed by adding 0.13 mL of 10% thiourea in order to remove excess bromine. Subsequently, 1 mL of 2, 4-DNPH solution was added, and all the standards, samples and blank solutions were incubated at 37 °C temperature for h in a water bath (Isotemp 120, Fisher Scientific, Canada), to check for the formation of osazone. After cooling the samples in an ice bath for 30 min, the samples were treated with 5 mL of chilled 85% H2SO4, with constant stirring. The resultant red color complex of osazone was recorded at 521 nm.
15: <header>2.5. Scenarios </header>

In this study, twelve treatments (12 scenarios) were defined, based on the different level of organic fertilizers (from Azolla) and water stress levels as two factors affecting product performance. These scenarios were defined to elucidate the role that fertilizer and irrigation play in creating environmental impacts of green- house tomato production, as well as the environmental impacts on the production of each unit of vitamin C in tomatoes. Table 1 summarizes the specifications of the scenarios discussed.
2.6. Life cycle assessment

The present study is an LCA where the environmental the im- pacts of the life cycle of organic greenhouse tomato production (the quantity of vitamin C content in it) was analyzed. This LCA followed the ISO 14044 guidelines (ISO, 2006a).
The first step in LCA, according to ISO 14044 guidelines, is goal and scope definition. In this study, the main goal was to investigate the environmental performance of organic greenhouse tomatoes
Table 1 Description of defined and studied scenarios.
*Field Capacity. a Levels of drought stress. b Concentrations of Azolla extract.
and its vitamin C content, based on organic fertilizers derived from Azolla, as a nitrogen-fixing plant providing plant nutrients for to- mato production, as well as water stress levels.

To reach this goal, well defined system boundaries included all inputs from cradle to gate of tomato until packaging. Represents a schematic view of the boundaries of the greenhouse tomato pro- duction system. It should be noted that the cut-off determination of LCA system boundaries was proposed based on the primary sources and their contributions to emissions.

Also, functional unit (FU) in the current study was one tonne of packed tomato fruit. Meanwhile, for information on environmental hazards, each unit of vitamin C (mg/100g) in tomato was selected as a FU for a more relevant conclusion.

The second phase of LCA is life cycle inventory (LCI) (ISO, 2006b; Altun-Ciftcioglu et al., 2016). In the present survey, two data sets were analyzed. The first group of data was related to foreground system and was collected directly in tomato production, including the amount of inputs and outputs as well as the amount of released emissions through the life cycle of organic greenhouse tomato (Table 2).

The second group of data was related to the background system, which was taken from ecoinvent 3.2 databases in SimaPro 8.2.3.0 software, including production of electricity, packing materials, production of irrigation systems, the production of materials used in the manufacture of organic fertilizers, etc. After all the data collection and calculations, the two data sets were represented after converting to per FU.

Emissions resulting from the application of fertilizers are one of the main factors in the production of harmful substances in agri- culture. In this study, the main fertilizers in organic tomato pro- duction were manure and organic fertilizers from Azolla that are N- based. The emissions of N20, NH3, and NOx into the air due to the use of manure and organic fertilizers were modeled using IPCC guidelines (Gallejones et al., 2015).

A. Emissions from fertilizers||(IPCC, 2006) (1)|kg N20 N kg Nin applied|0.01 (to air) (2)|kg NH3 -N kg Nin applied|0.2 (to air) (3)|kg N kg Nin organic applied|0.3 (to water) (4)|kg Phosphorus emission kg Phosphorus organic applied|0.05 (to water) B.|Indirect N20 from atmospheric deposition of fertilizers|(IPCC, 2006) (5)|kg N20 N kg Nin organic applied|0.01 X 0.2 (to air) C. Conversion of emissions||(IPCC, 2006) (6)|Conversion from kg N20-N to kg N20|44 28 (7)|Conversion from kg NH3-N to kg NH3|(17) 14 (8)|Conversion from kg NO-N to kg NO3|62 14 (9)|Conversion from kg P2O5 to kg phosphorus|62 142 D. Direct NO emissions from fertilizers and soil|D. Direct NO emissions from fertilizers and soil|D. Direct NO emissions from fertilizers and soil (10)|kg NOx kg fertilisers and soil|(IPCC, 2006)

It should be noted that, since organic fertilizer derived from Azolla was used as a foliar solution, and thus minimized fertilizer leaching, the effects of using this fertilizer on water quality have been considered equal to 0 and only the effects of manure appli- cation on water have been calculated.

In life cycle impact assessment (LCIA), as the third stage of LCA, the amount and significance of the potential environmental effects are identified and evaluated, using data from the previous step (data inventory), which is comprised of several elements, such as selection of the impact categories, category indicators and charac- terization models; classification; characterization; normalization (optional); grouping (optional); weighting (optional) and data quality analysis (optional) (Mimoso et al., 2015). A detailed expla- nation of the elements of LCIA can be found in ISO 14044 (ISO, 2006a).

Given the goals of this study, "ReCiPe method-egalitarian version" was selected as the method of impact assessment. It is based on a combined midpoint/damage approach and includes three damage categories. In the present study, results in terms of the damage categories (i.e., human health, ecosystem, and re- sources) were presented to compare environmental impacts be- tween scenarios. Finally, the results were subjected to sensitivity analysis and represented based on the midpoints categorized for the best scenario.

The results were represented as weighted since it is easy to compare the environmental impact of different products or sce- narios. Weighted results suggest clearly whether a product's impact is higher than, lower than or similar to the alternate methods.

Finally, life cycle interpretation is a systematic method to identify, check, quantify and evaluate data from the results of the LCI and/or the LCIA. Results from the inventory analysis and impact assessment are summarized during the interpretation phase. The outcome of the interpretation step is the conclusions and recom- mendations set for the study. According to ISO (ISO, 2006a, 2006b), the interpretation should include:

<list>Identification of significant issues based on LCI and LCIA results of an LCA; 
Assessment of the study considering completeness, sensitivity and consistency checks; 
Conclusions, limitations and recommendations.</list>

Finally, the results were subjected to sensitivity analysis and represented based on the midpoints categorized for the best scenario.

The results were represented as weighted since it is easy to compare the environmental impact of different products or sce- narios. Weighted results suggest clearly whether a product's impact is higher than, lower than or similar to the alternate methods.

Finally, life cycle interpretation is a systematic method to identify, check, quantify and evaluate data from the results of the LCI and/or the LCIA. Results from the inventory analysis and impact assessment are summarized during the interpretation phase. The outcome of the interpretation step is the conclusions and recom- mendations set for the study. According to ISO (ISO, 2006a, 2006b), the interpretation should include:

<list>Identification of significant issues based on LCI and LCIA results of an LCA; 
Assessment of the study considering completeness, sensitivity and consistency checks; 
Conclusions, limitations and recommendations.</list>
20: <header>2.5. Scenarios </header>

In this study, twelve treatments (12 scenarios) were defined, based on the different level of organic fertilizers (from Azolla) and water stress levels as two factors affecting product performance. These scenarios were defined to elucidate the role that fertilizer and irrigation play in creating environmental impacts of green- house tomato production, as well as the environmental impacts on the production of each unit of vitamin C in tomatoes. Table 1 summarizes the specifications of the scenarios discussed.
2.6. Life cycle assessment

The present study is an LCA where the environmental the im- pacts of the life cycle of organic greenhouse tomato production (the quantity of vitamin C content in it) was analyzed. This LCA followed the ISO 14044 guidelines (ISO, 2006a).
The first step in LCA, according to ISO 14044 guidelines, is goal and scope definition. In this study, the main goal was to investigate the environmental performance of organic greenhouse tomatoes
Table 1 Description of defined and studied scenarios.
*Field Capacity. a Levels of drought stress. b Concentrations of Azolla extract.
and its vitamin C content, based on organic fertilizers derived from Azolla, as a nitrogen-fixing plant providing plant nutrients for to- mato production, as well as water stress levels.

To reach this goal, well defined system boundaries included all inputs from cradle to gate of tomato until packaging. Represents a schematic view of the boundaries of the greenhouse tomato pro- duction system. It should be noted that the cut-off determination of LCA system boundaries was proposed based on the primary sources and their contributions to emissions.

Also, functional unit (FU) in the current study was one tonne of packed tomato fruit. Meanwhile, for information on environmental hazards, each unit of vitamin C (mg/100g) in tomato was selected as a FU for a more relevant conclusion.

The second phase of LCA is life cycle inventory (LCI) (ISO, 2006b; Altun-Ciftcioglu et al., 2016). In the present survey, two data sets were analyzed. The first group of data was related to foreground system and was collected directly in tomato production, including the amount of inputs and outputs as well as the amount of released emissions through the life cycle of organic greenhouse tomato (Table 2).

The second group of data was related to the background system, which was taken from ecoinvent 3.2 databases in SimaPro 8.2.3.0 software, including production of electricity, packing materials, production of irrigation systems, the production of materials used in the manufacture of organic fertilizers, etc. After all the data collection and calculations, the two data sets were represented after converting to per FU.

Emissions resulting from the application of fertilizers are one of the main factors in the production of harmful substances in agri- culture. In this study, the main fertilizers in organic tomato pro- duction were manure and organic fertilizers from Azolla that are N- based. The emissions of N20, NH3, and NOx into the air due to the use of manure and organic fertilizers were modeled using IPCC guidelines (Gallejones et al., 2015).

A. Emissions from fertilizers||(IPCC, 2006) (1)|kg N20 N kg Nin applied|0.01 (to air) (2)|kg NH3 -N kg Nin applied|0.2 (to air) (3)|kg N kg Nin organic applied|0.3 (to water) (4)|kg Phosphorus emission kg Phosphorus organic applied|0.05 (to water) B.|Indirect N20 from atmospheric deposition of fertilizers|(IPCC, 2006) (5)|kg N20 N kg Nin organic applied|0.01 X 0.2 (to air) C. Conversion of emissions||(IPCC, 2006) (6)|Conversion from kg N20-N to kg N20|44 28 (7)|Conversion from kg NH3-N to kg NH3|(17) 14 (8)|Conversion from kg NO-N to kg NO3|62 14 (9)|Conversion from kg P2O5 to kg phosphorus|62 142 D. Direct NO emissions from fertilizers and soil|D. Direct NO emissions from fertilizers and soil|D. Direct NO emissions from fertilizers and soil (10)|kg NOx kg fertilisers and soil|(IPCC, 2006)

It should be noted that, since organic fertilizer derived from Azolla was used as a foliar solution, and thus minimized fertilizer leaching, the effects of using this fertilizer on water quality have been considered equal to 0 and only the effects of manure appli- cation on water have been calculated.

In life cycle impact assessment (LCIA), as the third stage of LCA, the amount and significance of the potential environmental effects are identified and evaluated, using data from the previous step (data inventory), which is comprised of several elements, such as selection of the impact categories, category indicators and charac- terization models; classification; characterization; normalization (optional); grouping (optional); weighting (optional) and data quality analysis (optional) (Mimoso et al., 2015). A detailed expla- nation of the elements of LCIA can be found in ISO 14044 (ISO, 2006a).

Given the goals of this study, "ReCiPe method-egalitarian version" was selected as the method of impact assessment. It is based on a combined midpoint/damage approach and includes three damage categories. In the present study, results in terms of the damage categories (i.e., human health, ecosystem, and re- sources) were presented to compare environmental impacts be- tween scenarios. Finally, the results were subjected to sensitivity analysis and represented based on the midpoints categorized for the best scenario.

The results were represented as weighted since it is easy to compare the environmental impact of different products or sce- narios. Weighted results suggest clearly whether a product's impact is higher than, lower than or similar to the alternate methods.

Finally, life cycle interpretation is a systematic method to identify, check, quantify and evaluate data from the results of the LCI and/or the LCIA. Results from the inventory analysis and impact assessment are summarized during the interpretation phase. The outcome of the interpretation step is the conclusions and recom- mendations set for the study. According to ISO (ISO, 2006a, 2006b), the interpretation should include:

<list>Identification of significant issues based on LCI and LCIA results of an LCA; 
Assessment of the study considering completeness, sensitivity and consistency checks; 
Conclusions, limitations and recommendations.</list>

22: According to Vera et al. (2015) the goal of the interpretation step in LCA is to draw conclusions, identify limitations and make rec- ommendations for the intended audience. A summary of the results obtained from the environmental assessment of different scenarios is presented in Table 3 for damage categories of the ReCiPe method, egalitarian version. As presented in Table 3, Sc (1-3) in human health and resources damage categories had greater environmental compliance while in ecosystem damage category; Sc (1-3) had the least damage to the environment.

Significant differences were observed, in all the damage cate- gories, between Sc (1-1) and (1-3) with Sc (4-2) and (4-3). This suggests that irrigation reduced to one fourth of the conventional rate resulted in a dramatic increase in environmental impacts. This could be attributed to the fact that decline in agricultural water consumption will lead to the conservation of water resources, but in the production of a product such as tomato fruits, performance is sharply reduced and leads to increase of environmental damage per tonne tomato. Also, at the same level of irrigation, reducing of nitrogen-fixing plants led to improved levels of environmental damage without significantly reducing yield.

Finally, based on the probability distribution of the calculated weighted results, the uncertainty ranges of each damage category and total weighted environmental impacts for one tonne packed tomato were obtained (Table 5). At the LCIA level, uncertainties of LCIA results introduced by the various scenarios (in terms of irri- gation and fertilization) in the LCI data were determined, based on the uncertainty of LCI data expressed as a probability distribution at a significance level a = 0.05. Based on the normalized standard distribution, for one tonne of packed tomato, the uncertainty per- centages of specific damage categories are in between 14.96 and 18.24%. In addition, uncertainty percentage for total weighed environmental impact is 17.14%.

Tables 6 and 7, show the environmental impacts, according to mid-point categories of the cleanest Sc (1-3) and the most polluted scenarios Sc (4-3), in order to identify environmental hot spots for each input. The results in Table 6 showed that packaging had the greatest impact on environmental damage per tonne of tomato, due to the release of emissions during the production of polyethylene packaging containers. The use of lighter containers to lower the use of polyethylene material can decrease the carcinogenesis impact. A

similar effect was achieved with the use of polypropylene (PP) bottles. In another study, the use of HDPE (or PP) bottles VS. PET bottles led to a decrease of 60% in the environmental impacts in tomato juice production (Aganovic et al., 2017). However, in Sc (4- 3), consumption of electricity in major mid-points was the main contribution.

Also, the findings in Tables 6 and 7, indicate that the GHG emission was from 2.36 X 102 and 6.30 X 103 kg CO2 eq per tonne tomato packed for Sc (1-3) and Sc (1-3), respectively. In another study, the amount of GHG emission for non-packaged greenhouse tomato production was calculated as 2.07 X 102 kg CO2 eq (He et al., 2016). GHG emission per tonne organic greenhouse tomato 5.47 X 102 shows that the use of organic fertilizers based on nitrogen-fixing plants (Azolla) and manure in greenhouse produc- tion lead to a significant reduction in GHG emissions compared to production in organic greenhouses based only on manure. From Table 8, GHG emission was lower in organic production than in conventional production. Therefore, the use of nitrogen-fixing plants can be a promising option for reducing environmental damage, especially the phenomenon of global warming and climate change, caused by greenhouse products.

Table 9 Results of weighting from the production of a unit of vitamin C from tomato
Table 10. Results of weighting from the production of a unit of vitamin C from tomato

and compared for each unit vitamin C as FU (Table 9). The results indicate that despite irrigation and fertilization at the lowest level led to reduced yields [Sc (4-3)] and increased environmental im- pacts per tonne of tomato, that did not reduce the amount of vitamin C in tomatoes. This result emphasizes that tomato vitamin C will not change under organic greenhouse conditions, while irrigation and fertilization are low. The results of this section can be used as a template for the production of industrial vitamin C in the pharmaceutical industry.

From Table 8, GHG emission was lower in organic production than in conventional production. Therefore, the use of nitrogen-fixing plants can be a promising option for reducing environmental damage, especially the phenomenon of global warming and climate change, caused by greenhouse products.

and compared for each unit vitamin C as FU (Table 9). effect on yield, while in case of high irrigation; the effect of fertilizer reduction on causation is not particularly effective.

Finally, based on the probability distribution of the calculated weighted results, the uncertainty ranges of each damage category and total weighted environmental impacts for one tonne packed tomato were obtained (Table 5). At the LCIA level, uncertainties of LCIA results introduced by the various scenarios (in terms of irri- gation and fertilization) in the LCI data were determined, based on the uncertainty of LCI data expressed as a probability distribution at a significance level a = 0.05. Based on the normalized standard distribution, for one tonne of packed tomato, the uncertainty per- centages of specific damage categories are in between 14.96 and 18.24%. In addition, uncertainty percentage for total weighed environmental impact is 17.14%.

Tables 6 and 7, show the environmental impacts, according to mid-point categories of the cleanest Sc (1-3) and the most polluted scenarios Sc (4-3), in order to identify environmental hot spots for each input. The results in Table 6 showed that packaging had the greatest impact on environmental damage per tonne of tomato, due to the release of emissions during the production of polyethylene packaging containers. The use of lighter containers to lower the use of polyethylene material can decrease the carcinogenesis impact. A

similar effect was achieved with the use of polypropylene (PP) bottles. In another study, the use of HDPE (or PP) bottles VS. PET bottles led to a decrease of 60% in the environmental impacts in tomato juice production (Aganovic et al., 2017). However, in Sc (4- 3), consumption of electricity in major mid-points was the main contribution.

Also, the findings in Tables 6 and 7, indicate that the GHG emission was from 2.36 X 102 and 6.30 X 103 kg CO2 eq per tonne tomato packed for Sc (1-3) and Sc (1-3), respectively. Therefore, the use of nitrogen-fixing plants can be a promising option for reducing environmental damage, especially the phenomenon of global warming and climate change, caused by greenhouse products.

effect on yield, while in case of high irrigation; the effect of fertilizer reduction on causation is not particularly effective.

Finally, based on the probability distribution of the calculated weighted results, the uncertainty ranges of each damage category and total weighted environmental impacts for one tonne packed tomato were obtained (Table 5). At the LCIA level, uncertainties of LCIA results introduced by the various scenarios (in terms of irri- gation and fertilization) in the LCI data were determined, based on the uncertainty of LCI data expressed as a probability distribution at a significance level a = 0.05. Based on the normalized standard distribution, for one tonne of packed tomato, the uncertainty per- centages of specific damage categories are in between 14.96 and 18.24%. In addition, uncertainty percentage for total weighed environmental impact is 17.14%.

Tables 6 and 7, show the environmental impacts, according to mid-point categories of the cleanest Sc (1-3) and the most polluted scenarios Sc (4-3), in order to identify environmental hot spots for each input. The results in Table 6 showed that packaging had the greatest impact on environmental damage per tonne of tomato, due to the release of emissions during the production of polyethylene packaging containers. The use of lighter containers to lower the use of polyethylene material can decrease the carcinogenesis impact. A

similar effect was achieved with the use of polypropylene (PP) bottles. In another study, the use of HDPE (or PP) bottles VS. PET bottles led to a decrease of 60% in the environmental impacts in tomato juice production (Aganovic et al., 2017). However, in Sc (4- 3), consumption of electricity in major mid-points was the main contribution.

Also, the findings in Tables 6 and 7, indicate that the GHG emission was from 2.36 X 102 and 6.30 X 103 kg CO2 eq per tonne tomato packed for Sc (1-3) and Sc (1-3), respectively. In another study, the amount of GHG emission for non-packaged greenhouse tomato production was calculated as 2.07 X 102 kg CO2 eq (He et al., 2016). GHG emission per tonne organic greenhouse tomato 5.47 X 102 shows that the use of organic fertilizers based on nitrogen-fixing plants (Azolla) and manure in greenhouse produc- tion lead to a significant reduction in GHG emissions compared to production in organic greenhouses based only on manure. From Table 8, GHG emission was lower in organic production than in conventional production. Therefore, the use of nitrogen-fixing plants can be a promising option for reducing environmental damage, especially the phenomenon of global warming and climate change, caused by greenhouse products.

45: In view of the findings and results presented in this study, determined the environmental impact on the organic tomato pro- duced in the greenhouse in Quebec of Canada. For this purpose, the fresh tomato value chain was considered. The results of present study showed that although irrigation increases led to more water consumption and had direct impact on tomato productivity, the environmental impacts per tonne of tomato was sharply reduced, Also the change in fertilizer rate had less impact on environmental damage, So that due to the use of Azolla biofertilizer with maximum concentration, besides supply of nutrition (macro and microelements), vitamin and plant growth regulators, it was eco- friendly environment among other treatments. Change in irriga- tion and fertilization rate was effective on tomato performance, but that did not change vitamin C content in tomato and the environ- mental effects of producing one unit of vitamin C. So, the concerns about reduction in vitamin C of greenhouse in comparison with organic tomato production can be eliminated.
